Fig. 23. Linear approximation of max of two canonical forms (left) and two generalized canonical forms (right) [51].  
deviation of the exact maximum C ¼ max(A, B). Similarly to the  
linear Gaussian case, the approximation of the maximum of two  
generalized canonical forms is linear: the coefﬁcients ci and  
function fC are computed as linear combinations of coefﬁcients ai  
and bi, and functions fA and fB, respectively, as in (27). Fig. 23  
shows the linear approximation of the maximum of: (1) two  
canonical forms that depend only on one linear parameter (left);  
(2) two generalized canonical forms that depend only on one  
nonlinear parameter (right). The approximation of the maximum  
Cappr is represented by the green curve. The approximation of the  
maximum of two generalized canonical forms requires the  
computation of the tightness probability TA, the mean, and  
the second moment of max(A, B). Considering the nonlinear and  
non-Gaussian parameter variations ﬁxed, the expression for the  
generalized canonical form can be rewritten by combining the  
of each variable are sufﬁcient. This approach is practical for cases  
with up to 7–8 nonlinear and non-Gaussian variables. For higher  
dimensions the integrals can be computed by MC integration, and  
the overall approach rapidly becomes computationally expensive.  
Moreover, the approach [51] does not provide a solution in the  
presence of correlated non-Gaussian parameter distributions.  
Since the deviation from a normal distribution becomes more  
signiﬁcant when the non-Gaussian random variables exhibit  
correlation, it is crucial to accurately manage the case where the  
non-Gaussian parameters may be correlated.  
The work in [52] proposes a parameterized block-based SSTA  
algorithm that can handle both spatially correlated non-Gaussian  
as well as Gaussian distributions. The correlations are described  
using a grid structure similar to [38], which incorporates also non-  
Gaussian distributions. This approach works even for cases when  
the closed-form expression of the PDF of the sources of variation is  
not available, and it only requires the moments of the process  
parameter distributions. These moments are relatively easier to  
calculate from the process data ﬁles than the actual PDFs, and the  
procedure is based on a moment matching technique to generate  
the PDFs of the arrival time and delay variables.  
mean value a0 and the term fA(DXN)  
nLG  
X
A ¼ ða0 þ fAð  
DXNÞÞ þ  
aLG;iDXLG;i þ an þ1DRa.  
LG

i¼1  
Expression (28) can be considered as a canonical form Acond  
with a mean value a0+fA( XN) and linear Gaussian parameters. All  
D
To incorporate the effects of both Gaussian and non-Gaussian  
parameters in the SSTA framework presented in [52], all delays  
and arrival times are represented in linear form as  
the sensitivities are the same as in the original generalized  
canonical form (26). If two generalized canonical forms A and B  
are represented as in (28), the conditional tightness probability,  
conditional mean, and second moments of max(A, B) are functions  
of the nonlinear and non-Gaussian parameters DXN (with ﬁxed  
values) given by

where D is the random variable corresponding to a timing  
quantity (gate delay or arrival time at the input pin of a gate), Xi  
[Yj] is a non-Gaussian [Gaussian] random variable corresponding  
to the physical parameter variation, bi [cj] is the ﬁrst-order (linear)  
sensitivity of the timing quantity with respect to the ith non-  
Gaussian [jth Gaussian] parameter, Z is the uncorrelated para-  
meter that could be either a Gaussian or non-Gaussian random  
variable, e is the sensitivity with respect to the uncorrelated  
variable, and n [m] is the number of correlated non-Gaussian  
[Gaussian] random variables. In the vector form, B and C are the  
sensitivity vectors for X, the random vector of non-Gaussian  
parameter variations, and Y, the random vector of Gaussian  
random variables, respectively. Gaussian and non-Gaussian para-  
DXNÞ ¼ E½maxðA; BÞj  
XN,  
2
D
XNÞ ¼ E½ðmaxðA; BÞÞ j  
D
XN.  
The linear Gaussian parameters are independent of the non-  
linear and non-Gaussian ones. Therefore, the joint conditional PDF  
of the linear Gaussian parameters at the condition of frozen values  
of nonlinear and non-Gaussian parameters is simply a JPDF of the  
linear Gaussian parameters. Hence, the same approach presented  
in [37] and reported in Section 4.2 can be used to compute the  
conditional tightness probability, mean, and second moments for  
the maximum of two generalized canonical forms at the condition  
that all nonlinear and non-Gaussian parameters are frozen, by  
substituting a0+fA(DXN) and b0+fB(DXN) for a0 and b0, respectively.  
The unconditional tightness probability, mean, and second  
moment of max(A, B) can be computed by integrating the  
conditional tightness probability, mean, and second moment over  
the space of nonlinear and non-Gaussian parameters with their  
JPDF, where such integration can be implemented by any  
numerical technique. Although the computational complexity  
for numerical integration by discretizing the integration region is  
exponential with respect to the number of nonlinear and non-  
Gaussian parameters, the experimental results presented in [51]  
show that for achieving a reasonable accuracy 5–7 discrete points  
meters are statistical independent. The mean  
m
is adjusted so that  
X and Y are centered, i.e., each Xi, Yj, and Z has zero-mean.  
For computational and conceptual simplicity, it is useful to  
work with a set of statistically independent random variables.  
Since the random vector Y consists of correlated Gaussian random  
variables, a PCA transformation R ¼ PYY guarantees statistical  
independence for the components of the transformed vector R  
(for a Gaussian distribution, uncorrelatedness implies statistical  
independence). Such a property does not hold for general non-  
Gaussian parameters X.  
ARTICLE IN PRESS  
C. Forzan, D. Pandini / INTEGRATION, the VLSI journal 42 (2009) 409–435  
427  
Independent component analysis (ICA) is a mathematical  
technique that accomplishes the desired goal of transforming a  
set of non-Gaussian correlated random variables into a set of  
random variables that are statistically as independent as possible,  
via a linear transformation. The approach described in [52] uses  
ICA as a preprocessing step to transform the correlated set of non-  
Gaussian random variables X1, y, Xn to a set of statistically  
independent variables S1, y, Sn by the following relation:  
A quadratic timing model was proposed in [50] to capture the  
nonlinearity of the dependency of gate and wire delays as well as  
arrival times on the variation sources. In [50], the ﬁrst-order  
canonical model was extended with second-order terms:  
X
X
D ¼ m þ  
aR þ  
biXi þ  
aijXiXj,  
(31)  
i
i;j  
where aij are quadratic coefﬁcients and m is a constant term that  
in general might be different from the mean value of the delay  
timing variable. The difference with respect to the generalized  
canonical form (26) proposed in [51] is that in (26) the nonlinear/  
non-Gaussian parameters are represented by the nonlinear  
n
X
S ¼ W ꢁ X where Si ¼ WTi ꢁ X ¼  
wij ꢁ Xj 8i ¼ 1; . . . ; n.  
j¼1  
As in [38], the chip area is ﬁrst tiled into a grid, and the  
covariance matrix associated with the random vector is  
function fA(DXN), while in (31) they are characterized by the  
X
quadratic terms. The quadratic gate delay model is formulated by  
the second-order Taylor expansion with respect to the global  
sources of variation (evaluated around their mean value):  
determined. Using the covariance matrix, and the underlying  
probability distributions of the variables in X, samples of the  
correlated non-Gaussian variables are generated and are given as  
input to the ICA procedure, which produces as output the  
estimates of the matrix W and its inverse A, called mixing matrix.  
For a speciﬁc grid, the independent components of the non-  
Gaussian random variables must be computed only once, and this  
can be carried out as a pre-characterization step. Hence, ICA does  
not have to be recomputed for different circuits or different  
placements of the same circuit, and this preprocessing step does  
not impact the runtime of the SSTA procedure. ICA is applied to  
the non-Gaussian parameters X and PCA to the Gaussian variables  
Y, to obtain a set of statistically independent non-Gaussian  
variables S and a set of independent Gaussian variables R. By  
substituting the respective transformation matrices A and PY in  
(29), the following canonical delay model can be derived:

where B0 and C0T are the new sensitivity vectors with respect to  
the statistically independent non-Gaussian components S1, y, Sn  
and Gaussian principal components R1, y, Rm. The inputs required  
for the SSTA approach in [52] are the moments of the random  
vector X: mkðXiÞ ¼ E½Xki , which can be computed from mathema-  
tical tables if a closed-form PDF for the process parameters Xi is  
available, or from the process ﬁles. After performing ICA, the next  
step is to determine the moments of the independent components  
S1, y, Sn from the moments of the correlated non-Gaussian  
parameters mk(Xi). The moments E½Ski  can be used to compute  
the PDF (CDF) of any random delay variable expressed in the  
canonical form (30) using the binomial moment evaluation  
procedure proposed in [53], since this canonical form satisﬁes  
the independence requirement by construction. After computing  
the PDF and CDF of the delay and arrival time random variables  
expressed as linear canonical forms, the sum and max atomic  
operations of block-based SSTA can be performed to obtain a  
result in canonical form.

where Ri and Ci are the resistance and capacitance of the ith wire

fringing capacitance, Wi and Ti are the width and thickness of the ith  
wire segment, and N is the number of wire segments with equal  
length l. Truncating the Taylor’s expansion of (35) at the second  
order, the quadratic wire delay model can be expressed in compact  
form similarly to (34):

W0i ¼  
Wi À E½Wi and T0i ¼ Ti À E½Ti are random variables, which in  
general are not statistically independent to each other since  
interconnects usually span a long distance and these variables  
may be spatially correlated. Due to the nonlinearity of the wire delay  
with respect to the process variations of width and thickness shown  
in Eq. (35), the delay distribution of the wire will not be Gaussian  
even if the width and thickness are usually considered to be  
Gaussian [5].  
4.6. Quadratic timing modeling  
In order to accurately account the impact of non-Gaussian and  
nonlinear parameters, most of the recent papers proposed as a  
solution quadratic timing models. In [54] it was reported that a  
quadratic delay model matches the MC simulations quite well.  
Moreover, for any Gaussian random variable, the skew (third-  
order moment) is always zero; hence, and non-zero skew  
distributions cannot be represented in linear delay models. In  
contrast, under nonlinear delay models, non-zero skews can be  
expressed by quadratic terms.  
If there are q gate/wire delays in the input cone of the arrival  
time Da and there are p global sources of variation impacting the q  
gate/wire delays, the arrival time will be approximated by the  
following quadratic form:  
Da ¼ ma þ aTa ꢁ Ra þ BTa ꢁ Xa þ XTa ꢁ Aa ꢁ Xa,  
(37)  
ARTICLE IN PRESS  
428  
C. Forzan, D. Pandini / INTEGRATION, the VLSI journal 42 (2009) 409–435  
T
where random variation vectors Ra ¼ ½R1; R2; . . . ; Rq ꢂNð0; IÞ and  
Compared with the SSTA method based on ﬁrst-order canonical  
model, the extra computation complexity of the method based on  
quadratic timing model stems from updating the quadratic  
coefﬁcient matrix A at every arrival time propagation step. The  
number of quadratic coefﬁcients is limited by the number of  
global variations and is usually a constant. Updating matrix A will  
not increase the computation complexity since it only involves  
moment computation of quadratic timing variables which is not  
dependent on the circuit size. To sum up, the computation  
complexity of SSTA based on quadratic timing model will be the  
same as its canonical timing model correspondence. In [54] the  
timing quantities such as gate and wire delays, arrival times,  
slacks, etc., are represented in the following quadratic form:  
T
Xa ¼ ½X1; X2; . . . ; Xp ꢂNð0; RaÞ are mutually independent local  
and global variations. If every arrival time in  
a
circuit is  
approximated as a linear combination of its input gate/wire  
delays and all gate/wire delays have the quadratic delay form (34)  
and (36), then all timing variables in the circuit, including gate/  
wire delays and arrival times, will have the quadratic timing  
model:  
DꢂQðm;  
a
; B; AÞ ¼ m þ aT ꢁ R þ BT ꢁ X þ XT ꢁ A ꢁ X.  
(38)  
In [50] it was demonstrated that for a quadratic timing quantity  
expressed as (38), its mean and variance are given by

where X ¼ ðX1; X2; . . . ; XnÞ is the independent process parameter  
where tr{ ꢁ } means trace and equals the sum of the diagonal  
elements of the matrix. The distribution of the quadratic delay  
model (38) can be computed by means of its characteristic  
function, analytically derived in [50].  
vector with normalized Gaussian distributions N(0, 1) derived  
from PCA, A is a symmetric n Â n matrix that contains the  
coefﬁcients of the second-order terms, while BT is a 1 Â n vector,  
whose components are coefﬁcients of the ﬁrst-order terms, and C  
is a scalar constant term. Therefore, the sum operation of two  
random variables Y1 and Y2 is straightforward:  
If random variables X and Y are both expressed in quadratic  
form (38), the output of the sum operator is given by  
Z ¼ X þ YꢂZðmZ;

Y ¼ sumðY1; Y2Þ ¼ Y1 þ Y2 ¼ XT ꢁ ðA1 þ A2Þ ꢁ X  
In contrast, the max operator is intrinsically nonlinear, and it is  
necessary to evaluate if it can be approximated with a linear  
operator. The linearity of the max operator can be evaluated by the  
Gaussianity of the max output assuming the inputs are Gaussian.  
Skewness, which is a symmetry indicator of the distribution, can  
then be applied for the purpose of Gaussianity checking since a  
Gaussian distribution will always be symmetric. To propagate the  
quadratic timing model through the max operator, in [50] the max  
operation is ﬁrst performed on two Gaussian inputs whose mean  
and variance match what is computed from the quadratic timing  
model. Then, the equations given in [42] are used to compute the  
output skewness. If the skewness is smaller than a threshold, then  
the max operator can be approximated by a linear operator.  
Otherwise, both inputs are placed into a max-tuple (Mt), which is  
a collection of random variables waiting to be maxed. The actual  
max operation can be postponed, since the sum operation for a  
max-tuple can be simply done as  
þ ðBT1 þ BT2Þ ꢁ X þ C1 þ C2.  
(39)  
In order to simplify the max operation, the cross terms XiXj in  
the quadratic expression:  
maxðY1; Y2Þ ¼ Y1 þ maxð0; Y2 À Y1Þ ¼ Y1  
þ maxð0; XT ꢁ ðA2 À A1Þ ꢁ X þ ðBT2 À BT1Þ ꢁ X  
þ C2 À C1Þ  
should be removed, where Y1 and Y2 are expressed by quadratic  
forms as in (39). (A2–A1) is a symmetric matrix, thus it can be  
factorized as: PT ꢁ  
R
ꢁ P, where  
R
is a diagonal matrix composed by  
the eigenvalues of (A2–A1) and P is the corresponding eigenvector  
matrix. If Z ¼ P ꢁ X and  
U
¼ ðBT2 À BT1Þ ꢁ PT, then we obtain the  
following expression:  
maxðY1; Y2Þ ¼ Y1 þ maxð0; ZT ꢁ  
R
ꢁ Z þ  
U
ꢁ Z þ C2 À C1Þ,  
which no longer includes cross terms in the max operation. Since  
Xi’s are independent Gaussian random variables, then also Zi’s are  
Gaussian random variables. Moreover, since the eigenvectors P of  
MtfX; Yg þ D ¼ MtfX þ D; Y þ Dg  
and the max operation between two max-tuples is the merge of  
two tuples together:  
a
symmetric matrix (A2ÀA1) are orthonormal, Zi’s are also  
uncorrelated; hence, Zi’s are also independent [53]. Therefore, it  
is possible to map the original parameter base into a new base  
without cross terms, perform the max operation under the new  
base, and map the results back into the original base. Based on  
this orthogonalization procedure, the inputs of the max operation  
in the approach presented in [54] are quadratic functions of an  
maxðMtfX; Yg; MtfU; VgÞ ¼ MtfX; Y; U; Vg.  
To maintain the size of the max-tuple as small as possible, the  
linearity of the max operation is constantly checked between any  
two members of the max-tuple: if their max output skewness is  
small enough, then the max operation is performed on the two  
variables. With such conditional linear max operation, it is  
possible to control the error of the linear approximation for max  
operator within an acceptable range.  
T
independent normalized base X ¼ ðX1; X2; . . . ; XnÞ without cross  
terms, where all Xi’s are normalized Gaussian random variables  
N(0, 1). The quadratic approximation of the nonlinear max  
operation in [54] is performed by solving a system of equations  
obtained via moment matching technique. However, this ap-  
proach requires expensive numerical integrations.  
When two quadratic random variables X and Y expressed  
as in (38) are maximized with  
Z ¼ a ꢁ X þ b ꢁ Y þ c, the approximation parameters a, b, and c,  
are computed assuming and are Gaussian and using  
the equations in [42]. Hence, the quadratic timing

A
novel technique to model the gate and interconnect  
delay was presented in [55], where the authors proposed a  
delay model representation using orthogonal polynomials,  
which allows to independently computing the coefﬁcients of  
the max of two delay expansions instead of using moment  
matching technique as in [54]. Their approach is based on the  
Polynomial Chaos theory. A second-order stochastic process can be

obtained in terms of a common set of random variables by  
substituting the KLE corresponding to each random parameter of  
gate i in its delay expansion di. Once delays of all gates are  
obtained, it is possible to perform SSTA to compute the circuit

delay through the circuit, both the sum and the max operations  
must be deﬁned for the proposed delay expression. Given two

where the functions ci’s are the orthonormal basis, and depend on  
the random variables modeling the underlying process variations.  
If the process variations are modeled with Gaussian variables, the  
basis functions are Hermite polynomials. In practice, the series  
expansion in (40) is truncated to a ﬁnite number of terms. While  
for any general distribution of random variables and any arbitrary  
function f the coefﬁcients ai can be estimated with expensive  
numerical techniques such as MC or generalized quadrature  
methods, for some speciﬁc distribution such as Gaussian, Uni-  
form, etc., and a smooth function f, the integral can be evaluated  
with very high accuracy using N+1-order Gaussian quadrature,

x
Þ. The compu-  
tation of the max is based on an efﬁcient dimensionality reduction  
technique, which uses the moment matching methods to obtain  
the coefﬁcients of the max of two delay expansions. The  
computation of the sum and max can also be extended to non-  
Gaussian variables. Therefore, the proposed approach can be used  
to propagate linear expansions of non-Gaussian variables.  
Another approach where gate delay and arrival time distribu-  
tions were modeled as polynomials using a Taylor-series expan-  
sion on the underlying parameters was presented in [48], where  
the degree of the polynomial depends on the magnitude of the  
variations and the required level of accuracy. In this work, the gate  
delay is a function of location-dependent parameters that are  
mutually independent random variables. Suppose P, Q, and R are  
such parameters (although the approach is very general and can  
be easily extended to more parameters); hence, the gate delay can  
be expressed similarly to (24)) as  
where  
N
is the order of the polynomial that accurately  
approximates f. In [55] this method is used to perform library  
characterization; since standard cell delay and output slew can be  
modeled accurately using a second-order expansion, a third-order  
Gaussian quadrature can be used to estimate the expansion  
coefﬁcients.  
The delay is ﬁrst expressed as a multi-variate function of both  
the process variations (e.g., Vtn, Vtp, Tox, L), load capacitance Ceff, and  
input slew Sin, thus treating all these variables as deterministic  
~
quantities. By denoting with Z the normalized variables within the  
range [À1, 1], the delay deterministic model can be expressed as a  
~
second-order Chebyshev polynomial series in the variables Z. The  
D ¼ DðP; Q; RÞ,  
(42)  
coefﬁcients of the Chebyshev polynomial expansion are obtained  
from the third-order interpolation of Chebyshev zeros on the  
Smolyak grid, to ensure some optimality in convergence while  
reducing the number of interpolation points:  
where D can be a nonlinear function, and even if the random  
variables P, Q, and R are Gaussian distributions, in general the  
delay distribution (42) will not be Gaussian. Each parameter can  
be represented as a linear combination of the underlying random  
components as in (17), using the spatial correlation model  
described in Section 4.3. Therefore, expression (42) becomes

and for the sake of conciseness the random variables in (43) are  
represented with the following notation:  
i¼1  
Subsequently, the delay deterministic model is projected onto a  
second-order Hermite polynomial basis in the process variables  
and input slew. The coefﬁcients of the second-order Hermite  
polynomial expansion, which are functions of the load capaci-  
tance Ceff, can be readily obtained for various values of Ceff by  
using the Galerkin technique. As a result, the delay can be  
expressed as  
D ¼ DðX1; X2; X3; X4; X5; X6; X7; X8; X9; X10; X11; X12Þ,  
where all the random variables Xi are independent with zero  
mean and ﬁnite variance. The Taylor-series expansion of (44)  
around the mean values yields:

Þ,  
(41)  
where D(0) is the nominal value for gate delay (44) when all Xk  
random variables assume their nominal value. Expression (45) is  
similar to the quadratic gate delay represented by (32), and the  
gate delay is modeled as a general polynomial in the global  
variables Xk. It is worth pointing out that in (45) there are 66  
second-order cross terms in the form XiXj, with iaj:  
i¼0  
¯
where  
x
represents the normalized (zero mean, unit variance)  
process and slew variables. A similar approach is adopted for  
modeling the output slew.  
Due to manufacturing variations, some gate parameters on a  
die are random variables. Moreover, for a particular die, these  
random variables are functions of the gate location on the die, and  
D ¼ c1X1 þ ꢁ ꢁ ꢁ þ c12X12 þ c13X21 þ ꢁ ꢁ ꢁ þ c24X212 þ ꢁ ꢁ ꢁ  
(46)  
can be modeled as a stochastic process pðx; yÞ, where x ¼ ðx; yÞ is  
¯
¯
and consequently there are 91 terms in expression (46), which is  
the second-order truncation of the Taylor-series expansion (45). It  
can be observed that by increasing the degree of the approximat-  
ing polynomial, the number of terms increase and the error in  
approximation reduces. Therefore, there is a trade-off between  
runtime of statistical timing analysis and its accuracy. This trade-  
off can be controlled by the degree of the polynomial (46).  
Moreover, since all timing quantities in the circuit share the same  
global variables Xi, this approach enables to effectively capturing  
the correlations between them, similarly to the works [37,38].  
The result of the sum operation between arrival time at the  
gate input Ai and the gate delay Di approximated as a polynomial  
the location on the die, and  
y
belongs to the space of  
manufactured outcomes. Ideally, for each parameter, there are  
as many random variables as the number of gates in a die. In order  
to reduce the number of random variables, in [55] it was proposed  
´

quadratic form, called general canonical form:  
X
polynomial in the same global parameters. Likewise expression  
(9) the coefﬁcient of each term in the resulting polynomial  
is the sum of the coefﬁcients of the corresponding terms in  
Ai and Di:  
D ¼ d0 þ  
ðaiXi þ biX2i Þ þ arXr þ brX2r ,  
(49)  
where Xi are the global sources of variation, and Xr is the  
independent random variation. The Xi random variables may have  
arbitrary distributions with bounded values; they are assumed  
independent (if they are correlated, techniques like ICA [52] may  
be used to generate a new set of independent components) and  
centered with zero mean. To propagate the delay in block-based  
SSTA, not only it is necessary to efﬁciently compute the sum and  
max operations, but the timing results after each operation must  
be represented in the same general canonical form. Therefore,  
given D1 and D2 in the form (49)  
Di ¼ polyðX1; X2; . . . ; X12Þ,  
Ai ¼ polyðX1; X2; . . . ; X12Þ,  
Aiout ¼ Ai þ Di ¼ polyðX1; X2; . . . ; X12Þ.  
(47)  
Hence, the max operation among n polynomials obtained with  
(47) is a polynomial in the same global random variables  
Aout ¼ maxðA1out; A2out; . . . ; AnoutÞ ¼ polyðX1; X2; . . . ; X12Þ.  
(48)  
P
D1 ¼ d01  
D2 ¼ d02  
þ
þ
ðai1Xi þ bi1X2i Þ þ ar1Xr1 þ br1X2r1  
;
In [48] a regression-based strategy is proposed to compute the  
max operation by performing least square ﬁtting, trying to ﬁnd the  
best polynomial approximating the degree of polynomial (48)  
with the smallest error. To approximate Aout with a degree-two  
(i.e., quadratic) polynomial, the coefﬁcients of the approximating  
polynomial should yield the smallest error against the actual max  
operation result obtained on a set of sampling vectors for the  
parameter Xi’s. The advantage of using regression stems from the  
generality to handle timing distributions of any nature (not only  
Gaussians). However, the computational complexity of this  
approach grows exponentially with the polynomial order. To  
achieve the accuracy obtained from using a higher-order poly-  
nomial as well as runtime that is comparable to SSTA with linear  
delay models, a scheme using linear-modeling-based SSTA to  
drive the polynomial (i.e., quadratic) SSTA was proposed in [48].  
Although the quadratic polynomial can represent the PDF/CDF of  
gate delays and arrival times more accurately than linear  
modeling, the mean and variance of the distributions are captured  
with reasonable accuracy with ﬁrst-order polynomials. Therefore,  
in [48] a second-order polynomial modeling technique driven by  
linear modeling (which has lower runtime) was derived. With this  
technique the work presented in [48] avoided the complexity of  
solving a large (i.e., quadratic) polynomial regression problem at  
each gate (during the max operation) in block-based SSTA by  
solving a smaller linear regression problem and then performing  
moment matching (ﬁrst two moments).  
However, the proposed techniques to handle nonlinear delay  
dependency and non-Gaussian variation sources suffer from some  
limitations. The approach [52] addressed the non-Gaussian  
variation sources, but it is still based on a linear delay model.  
The nonlinear effects were considered in [50] and [54]: these  
works proposed a quadratic delay model. However, to keep the  
complexity under control they assumed that all the sources of  
variation must be represented by a Gaussian distribution, even  
though the delay may not be Gaussian. In order to compute the  
max between two delays D1 and D2, [50] treated D1 and D2 as  
Gaussians to obtain the tightness probability, even if there is no  
justiﬁcation why the tightness probability formula can be applied  
to non-Gaussian distributions. Instead, [54] proposed to compute  
the D ¼ max(D1, D2) by means of moment matching techniques,  
which requires several expensive numerical integrations. The  
works in [48,51] handled both nonlinear and non-Gaussian effects  
simultaneously. The ﬁrst one proposed to compute D ¼ max(D1,  
D2) by a regression-based strategy, while the latter dealt with the  
max operation through the concept of tightness probability,  
computed by means of expensive numerical multi-dimensional  
integrations. As a result, such methods are not suitable to handle a  
large number of non-Gaussian random variables.  
P
ðai2Xi þ bi2X2i Þ þ ar2Xr2 þ br2X2r2  
both D ¼ D1+D2 and D ¼ max(D1D2) must be represented as in  
(49). Denote  
DD1 ¼ D1Àm1 and  
DD2 ¼ D2Àm2, where m1 and m2 are  
the mean values of D1 and D2, respectively. Since both D1 and D2  
are timing quantities, their values are physically lower- and  
upper-bounded:  
ÀlpD  
D1pl; ÀhpDD2ph.  
To compute the max, the work [56] proposed a six-step ﬂow.  
The ﬁrst step computes the JPDF of D1 and D2, denoted as g(v1, v2).  
If the JPDF of DD1 and DD2 is f(v1, v2), it is easy to show that:  
gðv1; v2Þ ¼ fðv1 À m1; v2 À m2Þ.  
Then, the JPDF f(v1, v2) is approximated by means of K-order  
Fourier series  
K
X
fðv1; v2Þ ꢃ  
apqezpv1þZqv2  
,
(50)  
p;q¼ÀK  
where zp ¼ jpp=l and Zq ¼ jqp=h. In [56] an effective solution to  
simplify the computation of the Fourier coefﬁcients apq was  
developed: for an arbitrary source of variation Xi, the Xi’s range is  
divided into M small sub-regions, S1, y, SM. Then, the Fourier  
transform of the PDF of Xi, denoted as gi(xi), is pre-calculated for  
all pre-determined sub-regions of the variation source Xi, and the  
results are stored into a 1D lookup table. The valid region of each  
variation source is uniformly divided into twelve sub-regions and  
the fourth-order Fourier series is considered to represent the JPDF.  
t
In the second step, the raw moments Mt ¼ E½maxðD1; D2Þ  for  
D ¼ max(D1, D2) are computed. According to (50), Mt can be  
written as

DD2, and then by computing Eci,t, similarly to the derivation of  
Finally, the last three steps are needed to reconstruct  
D ¼ max(D1, D2) into the general canonical form (49), by ﬁrst  
computing the coefﬁcients ai and bi by matching E½Xti ꢁ  
maxðD1; D2Þ for t ¼ 1, 2; then by computing ar and br in (49) by  
matching the second- and third-order moments of max(D1, D2);  
ﬁnally by computing d0 in (49) by matching the ﬁrst-order  
moment of max(D1, D2). The computation of D ¼ D1+D2 in the  
general canonical form (49) is straightforward, both for the  
nominal and global random variable coefﬁcients, as they can be  
obtained by adding up the corresponding terms:  
A novel SSTA technique that efﬁciently performs the max  
operation and simultaneously handles both the nonlinear depen-  
dency and non-Gaussian distributions, was proposed in [56]. The  
authors represented the timing quantities in the following  
d0 ¼ d01 þ d02  
;
ai ¼ ai1 þ ai2; bi ¼ bi1 þ bi2.

Fig. 24. Application of RRR technique to second-order SSTA: conceptual (on the left) and actual (on the right) [57].  
For the uncorrelated random variable, there are two possible  
approaches. The ﬁrst one is to keep the correlation between the  
addition result with the two input uncorrelated random variables  
Xr1 and Xr2. The downside of this approach is that it causes the  
length of the general canonical form to grow longer after each  
addition. The second approach is to combine the two input  
uncorrelated random variables by matching both the second- and  
third-order central moments of the exact addition operation. The  
drawback of this method is that the correlation between D and Xr1  
and Xr2 is lost. Since the two approaches complement each other,  
in [56] it was proposed to choose the ﬁrst one when the  
coefﬁcients of Xr1 and Xr2 are larger than a predeﬁned threshold  
so that the correlation is not lost, and to choose the second  
technique when the coefﬁcients of Xr1 and Xr2 are small so that the  
form can be kept compact.  
t1  
t2  
An alternative approach to handle the increasing number of  
random variations, while maintaining the efﬁciency, was pro-  
posed in [57]. The approach is based on the linear reduced rank  
regression (RRR) that allows a powerful parameter reduction  
while considering the interdependency between parameters and  
the performances that depend on them. The conceptual applica-  
tion of RRR under the context of second-order SSTA is shown in  
Fig. 24 (left). For each circuit partitioning, RRR-based parameter  
reduction is performed once to reduce the number of local process  
variations and then a second-order SSTA can be carried out much  
more efﬁciently based on the original set of global variations and a  
reduced set of local variations. The way in which RRR is combined  
with SSTA is illustrated in Fig. 24 (right): RRR-based parameter  
reduction is intertwined with each SSTA processing step to  
dynamically control the parameter dimension. For strong sec-  
ond-order effects, the linear RRR framework can be extended to  
generate a nonlinear RRR regression model [58]. Results reported  
in [57] demonstrate that the additional cost of the RRR-based  
parameter reduction algorithm can be almost neglected when  
compared to the complexity of the second-order SSTA algorithm.  
t3  
t4  
Overlap  
Fig. 25. Statistical Static Timing Analysis with coupling: capacitive-coupled  
interconnects with their driver gates (above) and arrival timing windows in  
presence of variability (below) [60].  
the gate delay can be greatly impacted by the switching activity  
on neighboring wires. This change in delay due to capacitive  
coupling is referred to as delay noise and it contributes to a  
signiﬁcant portion (up to 40% stage delay error [59]) of the circuit  
delay. In traditional STA, the problem of delay computation in the  
presence of crosstalk can be formulated as computing the earliest  
and the latest arrival time among all possible waveforms of the  
aligned aggressors. The timing window for a given circuit can be  
computed by means of iterative algorithms [59]. In each loop, the  
early and the late arrival times at the primary inputs are  
propagated to the primary outputs taking into account the  
inﬂuence of aggressor gates. The resulting timing window of each  
net is compared with its aggressors to decide the aligned  
aggressors. The aggressor whose timing windows are not over-  
lapped with the victim net will be set as unaligned aggressor in  
the next loop to shrink the timing window. As shown in Fig. 25,  
the timing windows for two nets are overlapped if and only if  
4.7. Statistical static timing analysis including crosstalk effects  
Along with process variations, technology scaling to smaller  
dimensions also causes the dominant portion of wiring capaci-  
tance to be the inter-layer neighboring wire capacitance. Hence,  
ARTICLE IN PRESS  
432  
C. Forzan, D. Pandini / INTEGRATION, the VLSI journal 42 (2009) 409–435  
t1ot4 and t3ot2, where t1, t3 are the early arrival times and t2, t4  
are the late arrival times. Following this iterative timing window  
alignment procedure [60], proposed to extend SSTA to consider  
the impact of variations and coupling effects concurrently. In  
SSTA, the earliest arrival time and latest arrival time for a timing  
window of a given net become random variables. The overlap of  
two timing windows can no longer be simply determined by the  
condition t1ot4 and t3ot2, represented by the timing interval  
between the dashed lines in Fig. 25. On the other hand, since t1, t2,  
t3, t4 are all random variables, new random variables (t4Àt1) and  
(t2Àt3), along with the overlap condition can be deﬁned as  
follows:  
considering the uncertainty from variability ﬁrst. Hence, SSTA on a  
wire i computes a distribution of a single signal switching.  
However, when the uncertainty from functional information and  
input conﬁguration is also considered, the timing information on i  
is then  
a
set of signal switching distributions, which is  
represented by a window of signal switching distributions. In  
[61] the concept of statistical switching window was introduced as  
a representation for a set of random variables. For any random  
variable xni in the set, a lower and an upper bound on the  
probability that xni is not less (or more) than a given real value c is  
considered. The statistical switching window extends the bounds  
over the entire range of c, in the form of two distribution of  
correlated random variables xli and xui , respectively. These two  
random variables are called the bounding Random Variables of  
the statistical switching window xi. Mathematically, the statistical  
switching window is deﬁned as follows:  
mt  
mt  
þ 3st  
þ 3st  
40;  
4Àt1  
4Àt1  
40:  
2Àt3  
2Àt3  
By using the 3s values to determine the overlap of two timing  
windows, represented by the timing interval between the grey  
lines in Fig. 25, the proposed method prevents the over-shrink of  
the timing windows and preserves the earliest and latest arrival  
times. Furthermore, the correlations between different arrival  
times are inherently incorporated into the new random variables,  
thus removing any unnecessary pessimism in the timing window  
alignment. The mean value and the standard deviation for new  
random variable tiÀtj can be computed from the existing mean  
and covariance tables:  
xi ¼ ½xli; xui  ¼ fxni : Prðxlipxni pxui Þ ¼ 1g,  
where Pr(k) denotes the probability of event k. Then, the inclusion  
relation between two statistical switching windows xi and xj is  
formally deﬁned as  
xi ꢄ xjdefðPrðxljpxlipxui pxuj Þ ¼ 1Þ.  
Both a statistical switching window example and the inclusion  
relation between statistical switching windows are graphically  
illustrated in Fig. 26. The amount of delay noise is function of the  
overlap between the switching windows on the coupled nets. For  
two statistical switching windows xi and xj, the overlap between  
the windows is a random variable Oij deﬁned as follows:  
mt Àt ¼ MeanðtiÞ À MeanðtjÞ,

j

VarðtiÞ À 2Covðti; tjÞ þ VarðtjÞ.  
i
j
However, this approach estimates the switching window over-  
lap deterministically, based on the worst-case values, leading to a  
pessimistic computation of the victim delay variation due to  
coupling. An alternative solution to statistical timing analysis with  
coupling is to consider a distribution of switching windows on each  
wire of the circuit [61]. This view is obtained by ﬁrst considering  
the uncertainty from ignorance of functional information, and  
then the uncertainty from variability. Each window is denoted by  
Oij ¼ minðxui ; xuj Þ À minðxli; xljÞ.  
Since the overlap between two statistical switching windows is  
a random variable, the coupling induced delay noise is conse-  
quently a random variable.  
To compute the worst-case delay on a wire i when coupling  
effects are considered, we need to add to the delay on the wire  
when coupling effects are not considered, and the coupling  
induced delay noise due to each wire it couples with. In [61], an  
example of computation of a delay noise as a random variable  
based on a simple coupling model is illustrated. The considered  
coupling model is given by  
a
best and worst value. Therefore, the distribution of the  
switching windows contains the distributions of the best- and  
worst-case values. The two distributions thus obtained are  
represented as the distributions of two correlated random  
variables, respectively. The window formed using these random  
variables as the best and the worst-case value, respectively,  
contains all possible signal switching distributions. This transfor-  
mation of the original solution gives an alternate view of the  
solution to SSTA with coupling as a window of signal switching  
distributions. This view of the solution can also be obtained by  
(

DN no overlap;  
where DO and DN are the values assigned to D, depending on  
whether the statistical switching windows between the coupled

are expressed as a weighted sum of independent and orthonormal

skew distribution can be directly obtained by applying the basic  
theory of probability and statistics:

n
where r1 and r2 are the smaller and larger roots of the two  
quadratic pieces of the DCC. The delay noise in (52) is not  
necessarily Gaussian. However, using the PDF of delay distribution  
from (52), it is possible to compute the ﬁrst and the second  
moment of delay noise in closed form. Therefore, the canonical  
form of delay noise can be constructed by matching the ﬁrst two  
moments, while the correlations of the delay noise distribution are  
assigned by using the sensitivities of the given single aggressor-  
victim input skew distribution to process parameters. The proposed  
analytical technique can be extended so that the worst-case delay  
noise computation can be performed within the current SSTA  
framework with statistical timing windows, instead of single skew  
distribution. The solution is based on the result reported in [63],  
where it is shown that regardless of the aggressor transition, the  
worst-case delay noise occurs when the victim input transition  
occurs at the latest point in its timing window. Therefore, for  
computing the worst-case delay noise, in [62] only the distribution  
of late victim input arrival time was considered. Given the  
statistical timing window at the input of the aggressor, the early  
and late aggressor input arrival time distributions are subtracted  
from the late victim arrival time distribution to obtain the  
statistical skew window. The arrival time distributions of end  
points of the skew window are referred to as early and late skew  
distributions. As shown in Fig. 27, the skew window can align with  
the DCC in three different ways, denoted as Case A (when the mean  
of late skew distribution is less than the worst-case skew value if  
the DCC), Case B (when the mean of late skew distribution is less  
than z1 and the mean of early skew distribution is less than z1) and  
Case C (when the mean of early skew distribution is greater than  
z1). Since any skew distribution which lies within the skew window  
is feasible, for Case B, the delay noise is modeled by its worst-case  
value dmax. Note that in Case A, the DCC is a monotonic function of  
skew. Therefore, the mean delay noise will be maximized when the  
mean of the feasible skew distribution coincides with the late skew  
distribution. Therefore, the delay noise distribution in canonical  
form can be analytically computed as a function of the late skew  
distribution. Similarly, for Case C, the early skew distribution can be  
used to obtain the delay noise distribution. As a result, given the  
statistical timing window from block-based SSTA, the delay noise  
distribution can be analytically computed. Since it is in canonical  
form, it can trivially be added to the late victim output arrival time  
distribution and propagated downstream.

i¼1  
The computation of the di coefﬁcients as a function of the  
overlap random variable O is reported in [61]. While the approach  
proposed in [61] is extensible to an arbitrary coupling model, it  
cannot use Gaussian switching windows because the assumption  
of a Gaussian distribution for the bounding Random Variables  
prohibits the generic use of the inclusion relation between the  
switching windows. In this case, the solution is to replace  
Gaussian distributions with truncated Gaussian distributions for  
representing the bounding Random Variables. Arithmetic opera-  
tions on Gaussians are used identically for truncated Gaussians,  
although they involve some approximations.  
Another approach to include crosstalk noise into SSTA was  
proposed in [62]. Given a quadratic model of the delay change  
curve DCC which captures the dependence of delay noise on the  
aggressor-victim input skew, graphically represented in Fig. 27,  
and an input skew distribution in canonical form, the proposed  
approach allows to obtaining closed-form expressions of the  
resulting delay noise distribution. Since the correlations in the  
input skew are preserved exactly in the delay noise distribution, it  
is possible to express the delay noise in canonical form. Without  
loss of generality, the input skew distribution is given by  
s ¼ s0 þ s1x1 þ s2x2,  
where s0 is the mean, and s1 and s2 are the sensitivities with  
respect two independent standard normal random variables x1  
and x2. Since the process parameters are Gaussians, the input  
skew PDF fs(s) is therefore normally distributed with mean  
m
and  
variance  
s

s21 þ s22.

quadratic, as depicted in Fig. 27:

5. Conclusions  
As new silicon technologies keep shrinking the transistor size,  
it becomes more and more difﬁcult to precisely control the  
process parameters during fabrication. As a consequence, both the  
number and the magnitude of independent sources of variations  
are increasing. These unavoidable process parameter ﬂuctuations  
may signiﬁcantly impact the design performance, often resulting  
in a considerable parametric yield loss. Therefore, the accurate  
prediction of the process variation impact on circuit performance  
is a critical issue.  
Traditionally, the design performance evaluation in presence of  
variability has been performed either by running multiple STA at  
different process parameter ‘‘corners’’, or by verifying the design  
in the ‘‘worst-case’’ (‘‘best-case’’) corner. Due to the growing  
Fig. 27. Delay change curve captures the dependence of the delay noise on input  
skew [62].  
ARTICLE IN PRESS  
434  
C. Forzan, D. Pandini / INTEGRATION, the VLSI journal 42 (2009) 409–435  
quantity of process parameters, the ﬁrst approach may result in an  
unacceptable number of timing runs, while the latter may lead to  
overly pessimistic performance estimation.  
Acknowledgements  
The authors would like to thank the anonymous reviewers whose  
valuable suggestions improved the overall quality of the paper.  
SSTA is a promising solution to overcome these limitations.  
During SSTA, all the timing quantities such as delay, arrival time,  
and slack are treated as probability distributions. Therefore, the  
probability distribution of the circuit performance under para-  
meter variability can be predicted in a single analysis. Moreover,  
SSTA may accurately account for the actual process parameter  
distributions and their correlations, thus potentially avoiding  
overly conservative design. Furthermore, SSTA has many other  
advantages over traditional STA. For instance, it may provide  
information about the design sensitivity to different process  
parameters, thus driving designers to implement a more robust  
design. Moreover, it may predict the parametric yield curve, thus  
allowing an early decision making on risk management.  
Similarly to traditional STA tools, existing SSTA methods can be  
classiﬁed based on the different algorithmic approaches used to  
compute the delay distribution, i.e., path-based or block-based.  
Path-based analysis is accurate and can accurately capture  
correlations, but it suffers from important limitations: its  
complexity grows exponentially with respect to the circuit size;  
therefore, only a tiny fraction of the billions of paths typically  
present in modern SoCs can be analyzed. Moreover, the path-  
based algorithm is not incremental. On the other hand, the block-  
based approach has a computational complexity linear with the  
circuit size. By allowing the analysis to cover all possible paths  
simultaneously, it responds incrementally to timing queries after  
changes to the circuit are carried out. Furthermore, it provides the  
diagnostics necessary to improve the design robustness. Hence,  
even if the block-based algorithm is less precise and has some  
limitations considering the correlations, it is the engine that  
underlies industrial SSTA tools today.  
References  
[1] D. Blaauw, K. Chopra, A. Srivastava, L. Scheffer, Statistical timing analysis:  
from basic principles to state of the art, IEEE Trans. Computer-Aided Design  
27 (2008) 589–607.  
[2] D. Pandini, G. Desoli, A. Cremonesi, Computing and design for software and  
silicon manufacturing, in: Proceedings of the International Conference on  
VLSI-SoC, October 2007, pp. 122–127.  
[3] D. Pandini, Innovative design platforms for reliable SoCs in advanced  
nanometer technologies, in: Proceedings of the International On-Line Testing  
Symposium, July 2007, p. 254.  
[4] S.R. Nassif, Modeling and forecasting of manufacturing variations, in:  
Proceedings of the ASP-DAC, February 2001, pp. 145–149.  
[5] S.R. Nassif, Modeling and analysis of manufacturing variations, in: roceedings  
of the Custom Integrated Circuits Conference, May 2001, pp. 223–228.  
[6] L. Stok, J. Koehl, Structured CAD: technology closure for modern ASICs,  
Tutorial, DATE, March 2004.

performance under interconnect parameter variations, in: Proceedings of

the International Workshop on Statistical Metrology, June 2000, pp. 56–63.

parameter ﬂuctuations on the maximum clock frequency distribution for  
gigascale integration, IEEE J. Solid-State Circuits 37 (2002) 183–190.

in: A. Chandrakasan, W. Bowhill, F. Fox (Eds.), Design of High Performance  
Microprocessor Circuits, IEEE Press, New York, 2000.

variation due to Dopant ﬂuctuations, in: Proceedings of the VLSI Technology  
Symposium, June 1999, pp. 169–170.

variation impacts on ASIC timing, in: Proceedings of the International  
Conference on Computer-Aided Design, November 2004, pp. 336–342.

Semiconductor Industry Association, 2003.

of process variations in digital, analog and mixed-signal circuit design, in:  
Proceedings of the DATE, March 2006, pp. 387–392.  
[17] R.B. Hitchcock, Timing veriﬁcation and the timing analysis program, in:  
Proceedings of the Design Automation Conference, June 1982, pp. 594–604.  
[18] L.T. Pillage, R.A. Rohrer, Asymptotic waveform evaluation for timing analysis,  
IEEE Trans. Computer-Aided Design 9 (1990) 352–366.  
[19] P. Feldman, R.W. Freund, Efﬁcient linear circuit analysis by Pade approxima-  
tion via the Lanczos process, IEEE Trans. Computer-Aided Design 14 (1995)  
639–649.  
[20] A. Odabasiouglu, M. Celik, L.T. Pileggi, PRIMA: passive reduced-order  
interconnect macromodeling algorithm, in: Proceedings of the International  
Conference on Computer-Aided Design, November 1997, pp. 58–65.  
[21] S. H. C. Yen, D. C. Du, and S. Ghanta, Efﬁcient algorithms for extracting the K  
most critical paths in timing analysis, in: Proceedings of the Design  
Automation Conference, June 1989, pp. 649–654.  
Most of the recent research activity on SSTA has been devoted  
to mitigate the block-based approach limitations, i.e., the realistic  
correlation handling and accurate delay calculation. In this survey,  
the main approaches proposed in the literature addressing these  
challenges have been analyzed and discussed.  
One of the most signiﬁcant novelties is the canonical ﬁrst-order  
delay model that allows to considering both global correlations  
and independent randomness. By means of this delay model, the  
global and the local criticality probabilities can be computed,  
which are useful for design diagnostics.  
Along with the canonical ﬁrst-order model, the concept of  
tightness probability has been introduced, providing an improve-  
ment to accurately and efﬁciently compute the max of n correlated  
Gaussian distributions, which was a fundamental source of error  
inherent with the block-based approach.  
Several works have addressed the spatial correlation modeling,  
since ignoring such correlations may result in an underestimation  
of the variability impact. The main approaches, quad-tree  
partitioning, grid-die partitioning, and grid-based radial modeling  
have been analyzed and compared.  
[22] T. Kirkpatrick, N. Clark, PERT as an aid to logic design, IBM J. Res. Dev. 10  
(1966) 135–141.  
[23] B. Choi, D.M.H. Walker, Timing analysis of combinational circuits including  
capacitive coupling and statistical process variation, in: Proceedings of the  
VLSI Test Symposium, April 2000, pp. 49–54.  
Most of the block-based approaches proposed in the past  
assumed that all parameters had normal Gaussian probability  
distributions and affected gate delays linearly. However, some  
process parameters have signiﬁcantly non-Gaussian probability  
distributions. Moreover, as the process variations are becoming  
larger, the linear approximation is not accurate enough. Therefore,  
a few techniques have been proposed in the literature in order to  
include non-Gaussian and nonlinear parameters in SSTA. In this  
survey, the generalized canonical delay form, the quadratic, and the  
polynomial timing delay models have been discussed.  
[24] S. Tasiran, A. Demir, Smart Monte Carlo for yield estimation, in: Proceedings  
of the International Workshop on Timing Issues, February 2006.  
[25] R. Kanj, R. Joshi, S. Nassif, Mixture importance sampling and its application  
to the analysis of SRAM designs in the presence of rare failure events,  
in: Proceedings of the Design Automation Conference, June 2006,  
pp. 69–72.  
[26] S.R. Naidu, Speeding up Monte Carlo simulation for statistical timing analysis  
of digital integrated circuits, in: Proceedings of the International Conference  
on VLSI Design, January 2007, pp. 265–270.  
[27] V. Veetil, D. Sylvester, D. Blaauw, Criticality aware latin hypercube sampling  
for efﬁcient statistical timing analysis, in: Proceedings of the International  
Workshop on Timing Issues, February 2007.  
[28] A. Singhee, R.A. Ruthenbar, From ﬁnance to ﬂip ﬂop: a study of fast Quasi-  
Monte Carlo methods from computational ﬁnance applied to statistical  
circuit analysis, in: Proceedings of the International Symposium on Quality  
Electronic Design, March 2007, pp. 685–692.  
Finally, some techniques for including the impact of crosstalk  
noise into the statistical timing analysis algorithms have been  
described and compared.  
ARTICLE IN PRESS  
C. Forzan, D. Pandini / INTEGRATION, the VLSI journal 42 (2009) 409–435  
435

Cristiano Forzan received the Dr. Eng. degree in  
electronics engineering from the University of Padova,  
Italy, in 1993. In 1994 he joined STMicroelectronics in  
Agrate Brianza, Italy, where he is a CAD Expert. He has  
published several papers in his research areas, which  
include delay calculation, digital standard cell char-  
acterization, interconnect characterization and model-  
ing, crosstalk- and noise-aware timing analysis.  
Presently his research interests are in statistical  
analysis and optimization, variability-aware design,  
DFM for nanometer technologies, and EMC-aware  
design. In 2008 he received the ST Corporate STAR  
Gold Award for participating to the R&D excellence  
team on EMC-aware design.  
[47] V. Mehrotra, S.L. Sam, D. Boning, A. Chandrakasan, R. Vallishayee, S. Nassif, A  
methodology for modeling the effects of systematic within-die interconnect  
and device variation on circuit performance, in: Proceedings of the Design  
Automation Conference, June 2000, pp. 172–175.  
[48] V. Khandelwal, A. Srivastava, A general framework for accurate statistical  
timing analysis considering correlations, in: Proceedings of the Design  
Automation Conference, June 2005, pp. 89–94.  
[49] B. Cline, K. Chopra, D. Blaauw, Y. Cao, Analysis and modeling of CD variation  
for statistical static timing, in: Proceedings of the International Conference on  
Computer-Aided Design, November 2006, pp. 60–66.  
[50] L. Zhang, W. Chen, Y. Hu, J.A. Gubner, C.C.-P. Chen, Correlation-preserved non-  
Gaussian statistical timing analysis with quadratic timing model, in:  
Proceedings of the Design Automation Conference, June 2005, pp. 83–88.  
[51] H. Chang, V. Zolotov, S. Narayan, C. Visweswariah, Parameterized block-based  
statistical timing analysis with non-Gaussian parameters, nonlinear delay  
functions, in: Proceedings of the Design Automation Conference, June 2005,  
pp. 71–76.  
Davide Pandini holds a Ph.D. degree in electrical and  
computer engineering from Carnegie Mellon Univer-  
sity, Pittsburgh, PA. He was a research intern at Philips  
Research Labs. in Eindhoven, the Netherlands, and at  
Digital Equipment Corp., Western Research Labs. in  
Palo Alto, CA. He joined STMicroelectronics in Agrate  
Brianza, Italy, in 1995, where he is a Design Methodol-  
ogies R&D manager and  
a
senior member of the  
technical staff. His current research interests include  
signal integrity and interconnect modeling for DSM  
technologies, statistical analysis and optimization,  
asynchronous design, DFM and regular design, EMC/  
EMI. Dr. Pandini has authored and coauthored more  
than forty papers in international journals and conference proceedings, and during  
the academic years from 1998 to 2000, he was a visiting professor at the University  
of Brescia, Italy. He serves on the program committee of international conferences  
such as DAC, GLSVLSI, EMC-COMPO, PATMOS, ASYNC, and ESSDERC. Dr. Pandini  
received the ST Corporate STAR 2008 Gold Award for leading the R&D excellence  
team on EMC-aware design.  
[52] J. Singh, S. Sapatnekar, Statistical timing analysis with correlated non-  
Gaussian parameters using independent component analysis, in: Proceedings  
of the Design Automation Conference, July 2006, pp. 155–160.
